summary: Deprecated audit trail for svchost service splitting. The current app writes HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB = 0xFFFFFFFF to keep svchost service splitting from triggering on normal systems, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that publishes 0xFFFFFFFF as the supported disable-splitting preset.

setting.name: Service Host Splitting
setting.casual_explanation: Modern Windows can split services into more separate svchost.exe processes on systems with enough RAM. That improves isolation and reliability, but it also means more service host processes are running.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when they want older-style grouped service hosts with fewer svchost.exe processes, usually to match old tuning advice or compare memory behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling service splitting can reduce service isolation and make troubleshooting harder when one service host crashes or misbehaves. Microsoft's official docs describe the splitting feature, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft publication for the exact 0xFFFFFFFF override the app uses.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents svchost service refactoring and explains that service splitting on client systems is tied to the amount of physical memory available. The current app writes SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB = 4294967295, which effectively pushes the split threshold beyond ordinary system RAM sizes. Repo notes and Windows Internals references describe this as a way to keep services grouped, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that publishes the max-DWORD override as an official supported preset.

allowed_values.label: Official default threshold not fully captured
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents the memory-threshold concept for service splitting, but this dataset does not yet publish a fully validated raw default value for all variants.

allowed_values.label: Observed app no-splitting profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app uses the maximum DWORD threshold so normal systems will not meet the split condition.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app targets the official svchost splitting control surface, but this research pass has not yet captured a primary Microsoft source that publishes 0xFFFFFFFF as the supported disable preset.

windows_defaults.label: Modern Windows baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems using the standard svchost service-refactoring behavior

states.rationale: The official memory-threshold behavior is documented, but this dataset is not yet publishing a fully validated raw default threshold value.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps the feature documented without overstating confidence in a raw threshold preset.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

states.rationale: Do not publish or apply the max-threshold override until the exact preset is backed by a primary Microsoft source.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document service splitting without overstating confidence in the override value.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a validated Microsoft-backed no-splitting preset.
tradeoffs: The feature area is documented honestly.
The max-threshold override stays blocked from validated publication.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Observed app write used as the current no-splitting profile.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting what the app currently writes for service splitting.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a validated Microsoft registry preset.
tradeoffs: The current app behavior is captured.
The exact no-splitting preset remains based on repo provenance plus official adjacent docs.
recommended_profiles.notes: Blocked until a stronger primary Microsoft source for the exact override value is captured.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Service host grouping in Windows 10
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents svchost service refactoring, explains that service splitting depends on memory thresholds, and describes the general service-splitting behavior on modern Windows.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for service splitting
evidence.summary: The repo documents the SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB path, describes the threshold behavior, and lists 0xFFFFFFFF as the applied no-splitting override.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB = -1 (0xFFFFFFFF) for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft clearly documents the svchost splitting feature area, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that publishes the exact max-DWORD override used by the app.
blocking_issues: No primary Microsoft source for the exact 0xFFFFFFFF no-splitting preset was captured in this research pass.
The current app override still relies on repo provenance for the raw threshold value.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for the exact no-splitting threshold override be captured?
Should the app present this as an experimental service-host compatibility setting instead of a normal optimization?
